title: Refactor tax calculation to support regional pricing
body:
We need to clean up the pricing flow so we can support tax-inclusive regions for the EU rollout without forking the whole checkout path.

Plan is to add a `TAX_INCLUSIVE` flag and make `calculate_order_total` handle both modes.

For EU flows, tax is effectively already in the displayed price. For our current US/default setup, tax still gets added on top. I moved the pricing pipeline around so both cases can share the same code path.

Please take a close look at the order of operations here because it touches discounts, tax, and final total calculation.

comments_data.body:
I moved the tax step ahead of discount in `calculate_order_total()` so we can support the tax-inclusive case without splitting the pipeline in two.

For `TAX_INCLUSIVE=False` I believe the behavior is effectively unchanged, since we're still using the same subtotal and the default production path is tax-exclusive.

PR is up if anyone wants to sanity check the math.
LGTM from me. This matches what accounting asked for on the EU side, and I reran the existing tax-exclusive tests without seeing anything obvious break.
Thanks both. Merging this as-is. The default tax-exclusive path should still be fine.
Staging looked good for the EU scenarios we checked, so I'm closing this.

title: Discount amounts sometimes off by a penny
body:
We have a smaller pricing bug that might or might not be related to the bigger checkout issue.

On some percentage discounts, we always seem to round down instead of doing normal currency rounding. Example: 10% of $49.99 shows up as $4.99, but support expects $5.00.

Other examples we've seen:

- 15% of $33.33 comes out to $4.99 instead of $5.00
- 20% of $14.99 comes out to $2.99 instead of $3.00
- Clean numbers like 10% of $50.00 look fine

This feels like a rounding helper problem more than a discount formula problem.

comments_data.body:
I suspect `round_cents` here. We switched to truncation a while back to avoid float weirdness, but that also guarantees we shave off fractional cents instead of rounding them.

I didn't change it yet because I wasn't sure whether finance depended on the current behavior.
Checked with accounting and they are fine with normal currency rounding. We do not need to preserve truncation behavior.

title: Discounted order totals don't match expected amounts
body:
Flagging this separately from #15 because the checkout total itself is wrong, not just the displayed discount amount.

We're consistently over on discounted orders. Same tax rates, same promo config, but the final total is higher than expected whenever a discount code is involved.

Example from production:

- 2x Widget at $25.00 = $50.00 subtotal
- SAVE10 applied
- Tax rate 8%
- Expected total: $48.60
- Actual total: $49.14

A few more cases point in the same direction:

| Items | Subtotal | Discount | Tax | Expected | Actual |
|-------|----------|----------|-----|----------|--------|
| 2x Widget | $50.00 | 10% | 8% | $48.60 | $49.14 |
| 1x Gadget | $149.99 | 20% | 8% | $129.59 | $130.31 |
| 3x Gizmo | $29.97 | 15% | 6% | $27.00 | $27.37 |

Non-discounted orders still look fine, so this feels like either order-of-operations in the pricing pipeline or a smaller rounding problem getting amplified.

comments_data.body:
I checked the refactor from #12 and the tax-before-discount change was intentional for the regional pricing work. My assumption was that the default tax-exclusive path would still net out the same, so I didn't immediately suspect that.

Could still be worth proving though. The only other thing that jumps out is the `round_cents` behavior from #15.

I traced `apply_discount` and the percentage math itself looks fine. The wrong number seems to show up later in the pipeline.

My guess is we're dealing with two things at once: rounding is definitely not helping, but that alone probably doesn't explain a $0.54 swing on a $50 order.

Would appreciate another set of eyes before I keep poking at it.
